questions.question: Which of the following propositions is a tautology? A. (p \/ q) -> p   B. p \/ (q -> p)   C. p \/ (p -> q)   D. p -> (p -> q)
questions.to_find: Which proposition is true under every truth assignment

understand.plain: A tautology is a formula that comes out True for every possible assignment of T/F to its variables. The fastest tool here is the rule p -> q == ~p \/ q. Once you rewrite an implication this way, look for a variable appearing alongside its own negation, because p \/ ~p is always True.

keywords.explain: A compound proposition that is True for all truth values of its variables.
keywords.example: p \/ ~p is a tautology; it is True whether p is T or F.

keywords.explain: Any conditional can be turned into a disjunction: p -> q is logically equivalent to ~p \/ q.
keywords.example: p -> q becomes ~p \/ q, which simplifies truth-table checks.

keywords.explain: A single row of the truth table where the formula is False; finding one proves it is NOT a tautology.
keywords.example: For (p \/ q) -> p, take p = F, q = T: antecedent True, consequent False, so the whole thing is False.

xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='bg'/><rect x='140' y='55' width='120' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='78' class='sub ink'>p</text><text x='90' y='78' class='sub ink'>q</text><text x='150' y='78' class='sub ink'>p \/ width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='140' y='119' width='120' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='44' y='140' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='104' y='140' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='192' class='bg'/><text x='44' y='170' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='104' y='170' class='sub ink'>T/F</text><text x='192' y='170' class='ok sub'>T</text><text x='290' y='110' class='sub mut'>p \/ ~p = T,</text><text x='290' y='135' class='sub mut'>so column</text><text x='290' y='160' class='sub mut'>is all T.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A truth table showing that p OR (NOT p OR q) is True in every row because p OR NOT-p is always True.

given.aim: Identify the one option among A-D whose truth value is True for every assignment of p and q.

terms.meaning: Boolean propositional variables, each independently True or False.
terms.example: There are 2^2 = 4 assignments: (T,T),(T,F),(F,T),(F,F).
terms.connects: Every option is a Boolean combination of p and q.

terms.meaning: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False; equals ~p \/ q.
terms.example: F -> anything = T; T -> F = F.
terms.connects: Rewriting -> as ~p \/ q exposes the p \/ ~p pattern in option C.

terms.meaning: p \/ q is True when at least one operand is True.
terms.example: T \/ F = T; F \/ F = F.
terms.connects: p \/ ~p \/ q always contains a True disjunct.

given.plan: Rewrite each implication as ~p \/ q, simplify, and check whether the result reduces to a form containing p \/ ~p (always True) or has a False row.

steps.title: Test option C by rewriting the implication
steps.apply: Option C: p \/ (p -> q) = p \/ (~p \/ q) = (p \/ ~p) \/ q.
steps.note: Grouping is allowed because \/ is associative and commutative.

steps.title: Apply the excluded-middle law
steps.apply: (p \/ ~p) \/ q = T \/ q = T, regardless of q. So C is True in all 4 rows.
steps.note: T \/ anything = T (domination of True under \/).

steps.title: Rule out the others with one counterexample each
steps.apply: A: p=F,q=T gives (T)->F = F. B: p=F,q=T gives F \/ (T->F)=F \/ F=F. D: p=T,q=F gives T->(T->F)=T->F=F.
steps.note: A single False row disqualifies a candidate from being a tautology.

solution.result: Only C = p \/ (p -> q) is True for every assignment, so it is the tautology.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the rewrite p -> q == ~p \/ q, which turns option C into (p \/ ~p) \/ q and exposes the always-True excluded-middle law.

questions.question: What is the converse of the following assertion? "I stay only if you go." A. I stay if you go   B. If I stay then you go   C. If you do not go then I do not stay   D. If I do not stay then you go
questions.answer: A. I stay if you go
questions.to_find: The converse of the statement 'I stay only if you go'

understand.plain: First translate the English into a clean implication. The phrase 'P only if Q' means P -> Q (P being true forces Q). Here 'I stay only if you go' is Stay -> Go. The converse of P -> Q is Q -> P, so you simply swap antecedent and consequent: Go -> Stay, which reads 'if you go then I stay', i.e. 'I stay if you go'.

keywords.explain: 'P only if Q' translates to P -> Q: P can hold only when Q also holds.
keywords.example: 'I stay only if you go' = Stay -> Go.

keywords.explain: The converse of P -> Q is Q -> P; you exchange the hypothesis and conclusion.
keywords.example: Converse of Stay -> Go is Go -> Stay.

keywords.explain: 'P if Q' means Q -> P; the 'if' part is the hypothesis.
keywords.example: 'I stay if you go' = Go -> Stay.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 470 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Original: Stay -> Go</text><rect x='30' y='45' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='52' y='70' class='lbl ink'>Stay</text><line x1='120' y1='65' x2='220' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><polygon points='220,65 210,60 210,70' class='hi'/><rect x='225' y='45' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='258' y='70' class='lbl ink'>Go</text><text x='20' y='120' class='lbl ink'>Converse: Go -> Stay  (swap)</text><rect x='30' y='130' width='90' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='62' y='153' class='lbl ink'>Go</text><line x1='120' y1='147' x2='220' y2='147' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><polygon points='220,147 210,142 210,152' class='ok'/><rect x='225' y='130' width='90' height='34' class='hi'/><text x='248' y='153' class='lbl ink'>Stay</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An arrow diagram showing the original implication Stay to Go, then its converse with arrow reversed to Go to Stay, meaning 'I stay if you go'.

given.aim: Find the statement obtained by reversing the implication hidden in 'I stay only if you go'.

terms.meaning: The proposition 'I stay'.
terms.example: True when the speaker remains.
terms.connects: It is the hypothesis of the original 'only if' implication.

terms.meaning: The proposition 'you go'.
terms.example: True when the listener leaves.
terms.connects: It is the conclusion of the original implication.

terms.meaning: The arrow that encodes which proposition forces which.
terms.example: Stay -> Go for the original; Go -> Stay for the converse.
terms.connects: Swapping this arrow produces the converse.

given.plan: Translate 'only if' into Stay -> Go, form the converse by swapping to Go -> Stay, then phrase Go -> Stay back into English.

steps.title: Translate the English to logic
steps.formula_raw: P only if Q == P -> Q
steps.apply: 'I stay only if you go' becomes Stay -> Go.
steps.note: The 'only if' clause is the necessary condition, hence the consequent.

steps.title: Form the converse
steps.apply: Converse of Stay -> Go is Go -> Stay.
steps.note: Do not negate anything; only swap antecedent and consequent.

steps.title: Read Go -> Stay back into English
steps.formula_raw: Go -> Stay = 'if you go then I stay' = 'I stay if you go'
steps.apply: This matches option A. (Option C is the contrapositive; B is the original; D is unrelated.)
steps.note: 'P if Q' is Q -> P, exactly Go -> Stay.

solution.result: The converse is 'I stay if you go', which is option A.

questions.formula_note: The trap is the wording: 'only if' makes Stay the antecedent (Stay -> Go), and the converse simply reverses the arrow to Go -> Stay = 'I stay if you go'.

questions.question: Show that the formula [(~p \/ q) => (q => p)] is not a tautology. Let A be a tautology and B any other formula. Prove that (A \/ B) is a tautology.
questions.answer: The formula is not a tautology (e.g. p = F, q = T makes it False); and since A is a tautology, A \/ B is True for every assignment because A is always True, so A \/ B is a tautology.
questions.to_find: A counterexample for the first formula, plus a proof that A \/ B is a tautology when A is a tautology

understand.plain: Two short tasks. For the first, you just need ONE assignment that makes (~p \/ q) => (q => p) False, which disproves 'tautology'. For the second, recall a tautology A is True under every assignment; then A \/ B has a True disjunct in every row, so the disjunction is always True regardless of B.

keywords.explain: It suffices to exhibit a single assignment where the formula is False.
keywords.example: If one row of the truth table is F, the formula is not a tautology.

keywords.explain: A is True under every truth assignment of its variables.
keywords.example: p \/ ~p is a tautology, True in all rows.

keywords.explain: T \/ X = T for any X, so a True disjunct forces the whole disjunction True.
keywords.example: Since A is always True, A \/ B = T \/ B = T.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 470 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>Counterexample: p = F, q = T</text><text x='30' y='54' class='sub ink'>~p \/ q = T \/ T = T  (antecedent)</text><text x='30' y='76' class='sub ink'>q => p = T => F = F  (consequent)</text><text x='30' y='98' class='no sub'>T => F = F  => not a tautology</text><line x1='20' y1='112' x2='450' y2='112' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='136' class='lbl ink'>A tautology => A \/ B</text><rect x='30' y='150' width='110' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='52' y='173' class='lbl ink'>A = T</text><text x='150' y='173' class='lbl ink'>\/</text><rect x='180' y='150' width='110' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='173' class='lbl ink'>B = ?</text><text x='305' y='173' class='lbl ink'>=</text><rect x='330' y='150' width='110' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='362' y='173' class='ok lbl'>T</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Worked counterexample p=F,q=T making the first formula False, and a box diagram showing True OR anything equals True for the A OR B proof.

given.aim: Disprove that (~p \/ q) => (q => p) is a tautology, then prove A \/ B is a tautology whenever A is.

terms.meaning: Boolean variables used in the first formula.
terms.example: The assignment p=F, q=T is the witness we need.
terms.connects: They determine the truth value of (~p \/ q) => (q => p).

terms.meaning: A tautology: a formula True under every assignment.
terms.example: A could be r \/ ~r.
terms.connects: Its always-True value drives the A \/ B proof.

terms.meaning: Any propositional formula whatsoever, truth value unknown.
terms.example: B might be s \/ t, or even a contradiction.
terms.connects: Its value is irrelevant once A is True.

given.plan: Part 1: plug p=F, q=T to get a False row. Part 2: in any assignment A is True, so A \/ B = T \/ B = T, hence A \/ B is a tautology.

steps.title: Exhibit a counterexample for the first formula
steps.formula_raw: X => Y is False only when X = T and Y = F
steps.apply: Take p=F, q=T. Then ~p \/ q = T \/ T = T, and q => p = T => F = F. So the formula = T => F = F.
steps.note: One False row is enough to show it is not a tautology.

steps.title: Use that A is True everywhere
steps.formula_raw: A tautology => value(A) = T in every assignment
steps.apply: Fix any truth assignment v. By hypothesis A is a tautology, so value of A under v is T.
steps.note: This holds for all v simultaneously, the definition of tautology.

steps.title: Apply the domination law for \/
steps.apply: Under v, A \/ B = T \/ (value of B) = T. Since v was arbitrary, A \/ B is True under every assignment.
steps.note: The value of B never matters because T \/ anything = T.

solution.result: (~p \/ q) => (q => p) is False at p=F, q=T so it is not a tautology; and A \/ B = T \/ B = T in every assignment, so A \/ B is a tautology.

questions.formula_note: The proof rests on the domination law T \/ B == T: because the tautology A is True in every row, A \/ B inherits that True value no matter what B is.

questions.question: Let a, b, c, d be propositions. Assume that the equivalences a <=> (b \/ ~b) and b <=> c hold. Then the truth-value of the formula (a /\ b) => ((a /\ c) \/ d) is always A. True   B. False   C. Same as the truth-value of b   D. Same as the truth-value of d
questions.to_find: The constant truth value of (a /\ b) => ((a /\ c) \/ d) under the given equivalences

understand.plain: First decode the assumptions. b \/ ~b is always True, so a <=> True forces a to be True. And b <=> c means b and c always share the same value. Substitute these facts into the formula: with a True, the antecedent becomes b and the consequent contains c which equals b, so whenever the antecedent b is True the consequent is also True. The implication therefore can never be True-implies-False, so it is always True.

keywords.explain: b \/ ~b is a tautology, always True, so anything equivalent to it is forced True.
keywords.example: a <=> (b \/ ~b) means a <=> T, hence a = T.

keywords.explain: X <=> Y is True exactly when X and Y have the same truth value.
keywords.example: b <=> c forces c to copy b in every assignment.

keywords.explain: An implication X => Y is True when X is False, or when X is True together with Y.
keywords.example: If antecedent b is False the implication is True; if b is True then c=b=T makes the consequent True.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>Given: a = T (since b \/ ~b = T),  c = b</text><text x='20' y='62' class='sub ink'>(a /\ b) => ((a /\ c) \/ d)</text><text x='20' y='90' class='sub mut'>a = T  =>  (b) => ((c) \/ d)  =>  b => (b \/ d)</text><rect x='30' y='110' width='130' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='62' y='135' class='lbl ink'>b (=ante)</text><line x1='160' y1='130' x2='250' y2='130' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><polygon points='250,130 240,125 240,135' class='ok'/><rect x='255' y='110' width='150' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='278' y='135' class='lbl ink'>b \/ d (=cons)</text><text x='30' y='178' class='ok sub'>b True => b \/ d True; b False => implication True. Always True.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Derivation showing a forced to True and c equal to b, reducing the formula to b implies (b or d), which is always True.

given.aim: Determine the always-true value of (a /\ b) => ((a /\ c) \/ d) given a <=> (b \/ ~b) and b <=> c.

terms.meaning: Proposition equivalent to b \/ ~b, hence forced to True.
terms.example: a = T in every assignment.
terms.connects: Sets the antecedent and the first consequent term to depend only on b and c.

terms.meaning: Propositions tied by b <=> c, so c always equals b.
terms.example: If b = T then c = T; if b = F then c = F.
terms.connects: Lets us replace a /\ c by b in the consequent.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary proposition appearing only in the consequent disjunction.
terms.example: d may be T or F; it never affects the final answer.
terms.connects: Even d=F cannot break the implication because the consequent already holds when b is True.

given.plan: Substitute a=T and c=b to reduce the formula to b => (b \/ d), then verify this implication is True for both b=T and b=F.

steps.title: Pin down a and c from the assumptions
steps.formula_raw: b \/ ~b == T  and  a <=> T => a = T
steps.apply: b \/ ~b is a tautology, so a <=> T forces a = T. Also b <=> c forces c to equal b in every assignment.
steps.note: Excluded middle plus a biconditional with True nails a to True.

steps.title: Simplify the formula
steps.apply: T /\ b = b and T /\ c = c = b, so the formula becomes b => (b \/ d).
steps.note: Substituting c = b is the crucial reduction.

steps.title: Check the reduced implication is always True
steps.formula_raw: X => Y is False only when X=T, Y=F
steps.apply: If b=F the implication is vacuously True. If b=T then b \/ d = T \/ d = T, so True => True = True. Never False.
steps.note: There is no assignment giving antecedent True with consequent False.

solution.result: The formula reduces to b => (b \/ d), which is True for every assignment, so the truth value is always True (option A).

questions.formula_note: The decisive step is that b \/ ~b is a tautology forcing a = T; combined with c = b the formula collapses to b => (b \/ d), an always-true implication.

questions.question: Consider two well-formed formulas in propositional logic. F1: P => ~P   F2: (P => ~P) \/ (~P => P). Which one of the following statements is correct? A. F1 is satisfiable, F2 is valid   B. F1 is unsatisfiable, F2 is satisfiable   C. F1 is unsatisfiable, F2 is valid   D. F1 and F2 are both satisfiable
questions.answer: A. F1 is satisfiable, F2 is valid
questions.to_find: The satisfiability/validity status of F1 and F2

understand.plain: Simplify each formula with the rewrite X => Y = ~X \/ Y. F1 = P => ~P becomes ~P \/ ~P = ~P, which is True when P is False, so F1 is satisfiable but not valid. F2 adds (~P => P) = P \/ P = P, so F2 = ~P \/ P, the excluded middle, which is always True, hence valid (and so also satisfiable).

keywords.explain: There is at least one assignment making the formula True.
keywords.example: ~P is satisfiable: set P = F.

keywords.explain: The formula is True under every assignment.
keywords.example: ~P \/ P is valid; it is True for both P=T and P=F.

keywords.explain: No assignment makes the formula True (a contradiction).
keywords.example: P /\ ~P is unsatisfiable; F1 here is NOT this.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 470 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>F1 = P => ~P = ~P</text><rect x='40' y='42' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='42' width='90' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='52' y='63' class='sub ink'>P</text><text x='120' y='63' class='sub ink'>F1=~P</text><rect x='40' y='72' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='72' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='62' y='91' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='138' y='91' class='no sub'>F</text><rect x='40' y='100' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='100' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='62' y='119' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='138' y='119' class='ok sub'>T</text><text x='40' y='150' class='sub mut'>one T row => satisfiable (not valid)</text><text x='250' y='28' class='lbl ink'>F2 = ~P \/ P</text><rect x='270' y='42' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='330' y='42' width='90' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='282' y='63' class='sub ink'>P</text><text x='352' y='63' class='sub ink'>F2</text><rect x='270' y='72' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><rect x='330' y='72' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='292' y='91' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='368' y='91' class='ok sub'>T</text><rect x='270' y='100' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><rect x='330' y='100' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='292' y='119' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='368' y='119' class='ok sub'>T</text><text x='250' y='150' class='sub mut'>all T => valid</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two mini truth tables: F1 equals NOT P has one True row (satisfiable), F2 equals NOT-P OR P is True in every row (valid).

given.aim: Classify F1 = P => ~P and F2 = (P => ~P) \/ (~P => P) as satisfiable, valid, or unsatisfiable.

terms.meaning: The single propositional variable, True or False.
terms.example: Only 2 assignments to check: P=T and P=F.
terms.connects: Both formulas depend on P alone.

terms.meaning: Equivalent to ~P after rewriting the implication.
terms.example: P=F gives F1=T; P=T gives F1=F.
terms.connects: Has a True row so it is satisfiable, a False row so not valid.

terms.meaning: Equivalent to ~P \/ P, the excluded middle.
terms.example: True for both P=T and P=F.
terms.connects: Always True, therefore valid.

given.plan: Rewrite each implication via X => Y = ~X \/ Y, simplify F1 to ~P and F2 to ~P \/ P, then read off satisfiable vs valid.

steps.title: Simplify F1
steps.apply: F1 = ~P. It is True when P=F (satisfiable) and False when P=T (so not valid, not unsatisfiable).
steps.note: Idempotent law: ~P \/ ~P = ~P.

steps.title: Simplify the second disjunct of F2
steps.note: Double negation then idempotence.

steps.title: Combine for F2 and conclude
steps.apply: F2 = (~P) \/ (P) = ~P \/ P = T for all P, so F2 is valid. Hence F1 satisfiable and F2 valid: option A.
steps.note: Validity implies satisfiability, but the precise correct choice is A.

solution.result: F1 = ~P is satisfiable (not valid); F2 = ~P \/ P is valid. The correct statement is A.

questions.formula_note: Rewriting both implications via X => Y = ~X \/ Y collapses F1 to ~P (satisfiable) and F2 to the excluded middle ~P \/ P (valid).

questions.question: "If X then Y unless Z" is represented by which of the following formulas in propositional logic? ("~" is negation, "/\" is conjunction, and "=>" is implication.) A. (X /\ ~Z) => Y   B. (X /\ Y) => ~Z   C. X => (Y /\ ~Z)   D. (X => Y) /\ ~Z
questions.to_find: The propositional formula for 'If X then Y unless Z'

understand.plain: The connective 'unless' means 'if not'. So 'A unless Z' is read as 'A holds if Z does not hold', i.e. ~Z => A. Here A is the whole clause 'if X then Y' = X => Y. So the sentence becomes ~Z => (X => Y). Now export the antecedents: this is logically equal to (X /\ ~Z) => Y, which is option A.

keywords.explain: 'P unless Q' translates to ~Q => P (P holds when Q fails).
keywords.example: 'I go out unless it rains' = ~rain => go-out.

keywords.explain: (X /\ ~Z) => Y is logically equivalent to ~Z => (X => Y); you may bundle or split the antecedents.
An 'if ... then ...' inside another condition stacks two implications.
keywords.example: ~Z => (X => Y) is the literal reading of the sentence.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>"If X then Y unless Z"</text><text x='20' y='62' class='sub mut'>unless Z  =>  if not Z</text><rect x='20' y='78' width='200' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='34' y='103' class='sub ink'>~Z => (X => Y)</text><line x1='225' y1='98' x2='265' y2='98' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><polygon points='265,98 255,93 255,103' class='hi'/><rect x='270' y='78' width='190' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='284' y='103' class='lbl ink'>(X /\ ~Z) => Y</text><text x='20' y='148' class='sub mut'>exportation: ~Z => (X => Y)  ==  (X /\ ~Z) => Y</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The sentence rewritten as 'if not Z then (if X then Y)', then folded by exportation into (X and not Z) implies Y.

given.aim: Translate the English sentence 'If X then Y unless Z' into a propositional formula.

terms.meaning: The hypothesis of the inner conditional.
terms.example: X true together with not-Z forces Y.
terms.connects: Becomes part of the combined antecedent X /\ ~Z.

terms.meaning: The conclusion that should follow.
terms.example: Y is what is guaranteed when X holds and Z does not.
terms.connects: Stays as the consequent of the final implication.

terms.meaning: The exception introduced by 'unless'.
terms.example: If Z holds, the rule 'X => Y' is not enforced.
terms.connects: Enters negated (~Z) as a guarding antecedent.

given.plan: Read 'unless Z' as 'if not Z', producing ~Z => (X => Y), then apply exportation to fold it into (X /\ ~Z) => Y.

steps.title: Rewrite 'unless' as 'if not'
steps.apply: With P = (X => Y), the sentence is ~Z => (X => Y).
steps.note: 'Unless' guards the rule: the rule applies when Z is absent.

steps.title: Apply the exportation law
steps.note: Conjunction is commutative, so ~Z /\ X = X /\ ~Z.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.apply: This is exactly option A. Options B, C, D do not capture 'Y follows when X holds and Z fails'.
steps.note: Quick check: X=T, Z=F should force Y; only A demands Y here.

solution.result: 'If X then Y unless Z' becomes (X /\ ~Z) => Y, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: The key is reading 'unless Z' as '~Z =>' and then using exportation to merge antecedents: ~Z => (X => Y) == (X /\ ~Z) => Y.

questions.question: Determine whether each of the following is a tautology, a contradiction, or neither. ("\/" is disjunction, "/\" is conjunction, "=>" / "->" is implication, "~" is negation, and "<->" is biconditional, if and only if.) 1. A <-> (A \/ A)   2. (A \/ B) -> B   3. A /\ (~(A \/ B))
questions.answer: 1. A <-> (A \/ A) is a tautology;  2. (A \/ B) -> B is neither (contingency);  3. A /\ (~(A \/ B)) is a contradiction.
questions.to_find: Classify each of the three formulas as tautology, contradiction, or neither

understand.plain: Use the basic laws to simplify each formula before building a truth table. Formula 1 uses idempotence (A \/ A = A), so it becomes A <-> A, always True, a tautology. Formula 2 can be made False (A=T, B=F) and True (B=T), so it is neither (a contingency). Formula 3 expands via De Morgan to A /\ ~A /\ ~B, which contains A /\ ~A = False, so it is unsatisfiable, a contradiction.

keywords.explain: A \/ A = A and A /\ A = A; repeating an operand changes nothing.
keywords.example: A \/ A simplifies to A, turning formula 1 into A <-> A.

keywords.explain: A formula that is True for some assignments and False for others.
keywords.example: (A \/ B) -> B is True at B=T but False at A=T, B=F.
A /\ ~(A \/ B) = A /\ ~A /\ ~B, which is always False.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='440' height='52' class='bg'/><text x='32' y='44' class='lbl ink'>1. A <-> (A \/ A) = A <-> A</text><text x='32' y='64' class='ok sub'>always True => TAUTOLOGY</text><rect x='20' y='80' width='440' height='52' class='bg'/><text x='32' y='104' class='lbl ink'>2. (A \/ B) -> B</text><text x='32' y='124' class='sub mut'>A=T,B=F gives F ; B=T gives T  => NEITHER</text><rect x='20' y='140' width='440' height='52' class='bg'/><text x='32' y='164' class='lbl ink'>3. A /\ ~(A \/ B) = A /\ ~A /\ ~B</text><text x='32' y='184' class='no sub'>contains A /\ ~A = F => CONTRADICTION</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes summarizing: formula 1 simplifies to A iff A (tautology), formula 2 is True or False depending on values (neither), formula 3 contains A and not-A (contradiction).

given.aim: Decide for each of A <-> (A \/ A), (A \/ B) -> B, and A /\ (~(A \/ B)) whether it is a tautology, a contradiction, or neither.

terms.meaning: A propositional variable appearing in all three formulas.
terms.example: A is either T or F.
terms.connects: Drives idempotence in formula 1 and the contradiction in formula 3.

terms.meaning: A second propositional variable in formulas 2 and 3.
terms.example: B is either T or F.
terms.connects: Its independence from A makes formula 2 a contingency.

terms.meaning: Tautology = always True; contradiction = always False; neither = sometimes each.
terms.example: A \/ ~A is a tautology; A /\ ~A is a contradiction.
terms.connects: The label assigned to each formula is the final answer.

given.plan: Simplify with idempotence, biconditional, and De Morgan, then either recognize an always-True/always-False form or supply one True and one False assignment.

steps.title: Formula 1: A <-> (A \/ A)
steps.formula_raw: A \/ A == A,  then  A <-> A == T
steps.apply: A \/ A = A, so the formula is A <-> A, which is True for both A=T and A=F.
steps.note: A biconditional of a proposition with itself is always True: a tautology.

steps.title: Formula 2: (A \/ B) -> B
steps.formula_raw: X -> Y is False only when X=T, Y=F
steps.apply: Take A=T, B=F: (T \/ F) -> F = T -> F = F. Take B=T: consequent True so it is T. Both values occur.
steps.note: True in some rows, False in others => neither (a contingency).

steps.title: Formula 3: A /\ (~(A \/ B))
steps.apply: A /\ (~A /\ ~B) = (A /\ ~A) /\ ~B. Since A /\ ~A = F, the whole formula = F for every assignment.
steps.note: A formula equivalent to False everywhere is a contradiction.

solution.result: 1 is a tautology (A <-> A is always True), 2 is neither/contingency (False at A=T,B=F; True at B=T), 3 is a contradiction (A /\ ~A /\ ~B is always False).

questions.formula_note: Idempotence collapses formula 1 to the tautology A <-> A, while De Morgan exposes the A /\ ~A inside formula 3, marking it a contradiction; formula 2 has both True and False rows so it is neither.

formulas.name: Implication as Disjunction
formulas.whenToUse: To simplify or compare formulas containing implications, especially when checking for tautology, validity, or satisfiability.
formulas.trap: Do not negate q; only the antecedent is negated. p -> q is NOT ~p \/ ~q.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Logical Equivalences (Table of equivalences involving conditionals)

formulas.name: Negation / Excluded-Middle and Domination Laws
formulas.latex: p \lor \lnot p \equiv T,\quad p \land \lnot p \equiv F,\quad T \lor p \equiv T,\quad F \land p \equiv F
formulas.whenToUse: When a variable appears with its own negation, or when one operand is the constant True/False, to collapse expressions instantly.

terms.means: The logical constants True and False

formulas.trap: Excluded middle gives True only for \/ (p \/ ~p); with /\ you instead get the contradiction p /\ ~p == F.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Negation and Domination Laws

formulas.name: Truth Value of an Implication
formulas.latex: p \rightarrow q = F \iff (p = T \land q = F)
formulas.plain: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False; True in all other cases
formulas.whenToUse: To find a counterexample for a conditional or to verify an implication is always satisfied.
formulas.trap: A False antecedent makes the implication vacuously True, not False; do not mistake F -> F for False.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Conditional Statement truth table

formulas.name: Translation of 'Only If'
formulas.latex: P \text{ only if } Q \equiv P \rightarrow Q
formulas.plain: P only if Q == P -> Q
formulas.whenToUse: When converting English statements containing 'only if' into propositional logic.

terms.means: The necessary condition following 'only if' (becomes consequent)

formulas.trap: 'P only if Q' is P -> Q, while 'P if Q' is Q -> P; the two are converses, do not swap them.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Translating English Sentences (necessary vs sufficient)

formulas.name: Converse of an Implication
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the converse of a conditional statement; swap antecedent and consequent.
formulas.trap: The converse Q -> P is not equivalent to the original; only the contrapositive ~Q -> ~P is.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Converse, Contrapositive and Inverse

formulas.name: Translation of 'Unless'
formulas.whenToUse: When converting English statements containing 'unless' into propositional logic.

terms.means: The clause that holds in the absence of the exception

formulas.trap: 'Unless' means 'if not', so Z enters negated; reading it as Z -> P reverses the intended meaning.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, Translating English Sentences ('unless')

formulas.name: Exportation Law
formulas.whenToUse: To merge or split antecedents when comparing nested implications with conjunctive-antecedent forms.
formulas.trap: Only the antecedents may be bundled into a conjunction; the consequent stays single.

formulas.name: Idempotent Laws
formulas.latex: p \lor p \equiv p,\quad p \land p \equiv p
formulas.plain: p \/ p == p ; p /\ p == p
formulas.whenToUse: To remove duplicated operands in a disjunction or conjunction.

terms.means: Any proposition repeated in the connective

formulas.trap: Idempotence applies only to repeated identical operands, not to p \/ ~p (that is excluded middle).

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.latex: \lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q,\quad \lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q
formulas.whenToUse: To push a negation inside a disjunction or conjunction, flipping the connective.
formulas.trap: The connective must flip: ~(p \/ q) is ~p /\ ~q, NOT ~p \/ ~q.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, De Morgan's Laws
